judges: Coram: Her Honour Judge Elaine Liu in Chambers (Not Open to Public)

principles.paraphrase_en: Assets generated or acquired during a marriage using funds earned during the marriage are prima facie considered matrimonial property for the purposes of ancillary relief.

principles.principle_label: Beneficial ownership as basis for matrimonial pot
principles.paraphrase_en: A determination of beneficial ownership of property held in the name of a third party provides a useful prima facie basis for considering its inclusion in the matrimonial pot for distribution in ancillary relief proceedings. If a spouse is found to be the beneficial owner, the property should prima facie be included, and vice versa.

principles.principle_label: Timing for determining matrimonial pot inclusion
principles.paraphrase_en: The dispute over whether an asset should be included in or excluded from the matrimonial pot for distribution is better determined at the ancillary relief trial, after all evidence relevant to the statutory factors has been adduced, allowing the court to consider all relevant factors holistically.

principles.principle_label: Scope of trial limited by order
principles.paraphrase_en: The court's determination in a trial is limited by the scope of the issues as defined by the relevant court order. The court will decline to determine issues not covered by that order.

relationships.description: The judgment notes the practice adopted in this case of determining the matrimonial pot issue in the same trial as the beneficial ownership issue.

candidates.case_name: LN, by his next friend A, LM v. LK, The Executor to the Estate of LC, Deceased
judges: Coram : Deputy District Judge Jacqueline Lee in chambers (Not open to public)

principles.principle_label: Objective standard for reasonable financial provision
principles.paraphrase_en: The determination of what constitutes reasonable financial provision under the Ordinance is an objective assessment of what the claimant should reasonably receive, not an assessment of whether the deceased acted reasonably.

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof for claimed expenses
principles.paraphrase_en: A claim for a specific expense as part of reasonable financial provision must be supported by objective evidence, such as medical reports or fee quotations; a claimant's unsupported assertion is insufficient.

principles.principle_label: Relevance of deceased's lifestyle and expectations
principles.paraphrase_en: The deceased's established frugal lifestyle and the expectations they set for the claimant's standard of living (such as education at government-aided schools) are relevant factors in assessing what constitutes reasonable maintenance.

principles.principle_label: Best interests of a minor claimant
principles.paraphrase_en: Proposals for the maintenance and care of a minor claimant must be shown to be in the minor's best interests, and speculative or unimplemented plans may be given little weight.

relationships.target_case_name: Ilott v The Blue Cross and others
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from Ilott that reasonable financial provision is determined by an objective standard for the claimant.

candidates.case_name: CHINNEY SHUN CHEONG BUILDING SERVICES ENGINEERING LTD (formerly known as CHINNEY E & M (MAINTENANCE) LTD) v. LO KWAI FAN
judges: Before: Hon Au-Yeung J in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: A court has jurisdiction to provide further directions or vary an existing order for the sale of a property when there has been a material change in circumstances, particularly when the original order contains a liberty to apply clause.

A court may vary the minimum sale price specified in an order if presented with reliable evidence, such as a consistent valuation methodology, demonstrating a material change in the property's market value since the original order was made.

relationships.target_case_name: Lam Chok Lai v The Official Receiver and Trustee in Bankruptcy of Lam Chok Wai
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principle from Lam Chok Lai that the court has jurisdiction to give further directions for a sale due to changed circumstances under a liberty to apply clause.

judges: Before: Hon Yuen JA and B Chu J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A court will not make a joint custody order if the parents are so antagonistic that they would be unable to agree on most matters, as such an order would be unworkable.
The party seeking to vary an existing custody order bears the burden of demonstrating why such a variation is necessary.
The court must take into account the views of a child, particularly as they grow older, when making any important decisions concerning the child's welfare.

A child's relationship with a parent's new family is more relevant to access arrangements than to decisions concerning custody matters, such as education. The custody order does not determine the time spent with the child, which is governed by the access order.

principles.principle_label: Appellate interference with findings of fact
principles.paraphrase_en: An appellate court will not normally interfere with the findings of fact made by a first instance judge who has seen and heard the witnesses give live evidence.

relationships.description: The judgment cites the authority of PD in support of the proposition that a sole custody order does not prevent the non-custodial parent from inputting views or challenging decisions.

judges: Coram : Her Honour Judge Grace Chan in Chambers (not open to public)

principles.principle_label: Status of social welfare officer recommendations
principles.paraphrase_en: The court must give due consideration to reports from social welfare officers but is not bound to follow their recommendations; such recommendations do not constitute the court's decision.

principles.principle_label: Parental right to consultation on major matters
principles.paraphrase_en: A parent, regardless of custodial status, retains the right to be informed and consulted about major decisions concerning their child's upbringing, such as education, and may apply to the court to resolve disagreements.

principles.principle_label: Child's best interests as paramount consideration
principles.paraphrase_en: The court's primary consideration in custody matters is the child's best interests, and all evidence must be evaluated holistically to determine what arrangement serves those interests.

principles.principle_label: General costs practice in children cases
principles.paraphrase_en: In applications concerning children, the usual order is for no order as to costs, as the objective is to determine the best arrangement for the child, not to designate a winning or losing party.

relationships.target_case_name: Naziya Aslam v Ali (Custody and Removal)
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case to support the principle that the court is not bound by social welfare officer recommendations.
The judgment cites this case to reinforce the status of social welfare officer recommendations as non-binding on the court.
The judgment applies the general costs practice established in this case to the present children proceedings.

relationships.target_case_name: London Borough of Sutton v Davis (Costs) No.2
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case to clarify that the 'no costs' approach in children cases is a general practice, not a strict principle.

judges: Coram: Deputy District Judge J. Chow in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: The meaning of a clause in a maintenance agreement concerning a child's education is determined by its ordinary meaning, focusing on the act of enrollment rather than physical attendance.

The court may disregard submissions regarding the parties' negotiation history or how they arrived at specific financial figures in an agreement when such background is not relevant to the legal interpretation of the clause.

judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge To in Chambers

principles.principle_label: Extension of time factors for appeal
principles.paraphrase_en: When deciding whether to extend the time limit for appealing a refusal of leave to apply for judicial review, the court considers four key factors: the length of the delay, the reasons for the delay, the merits of the intended appeal, and any prejudice to the respondent if the extension is granted.

principles.principle_label: Obligation to notify court of address change
principles.paraphrase_en: A litigant has a legal obligation to inform the court of any change of address, including changes resulting from being taken into custody, and must bear the consequences of failing to do so, such as not receiving court notices.

principles.principle_label: Limited role of court in non-refoulement judicial review
principles.paraphrase_en: In a judicial review of a non-refoulement claim, the court's role is not to reassess the claim or evidence anew. Its function is limited to scrutinizing the decision of the primary decision-makers (the Director and the Board) for legal errors, procedural unfairness, or irrationality.

relationships.target_case_name: Jagg Singh and Torture Claims Appeal Board/non-Refoulement Claims Petition Office
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the four-factor test established in Jagg Singh for considering an extension of time to appeal.
The judgment applies the principle from Karamjit Singh that a litigant must inform the court of an address change and bears the risk of not doing so.

judges: Before: Hon Au-Yeung J in Chambers (Not Open to the Public)

principles.paraphrase_en: In proceedings concerning domestic violence, the court's focus is on determining whether the alleged conduct constitutes harassment or intimidation, not on adjudicating ancillary matters such as custody rights or division of matrimonial assets.

For the court to find that domestic violence caused psychological stress to a child, it must be established that the stress was caused solely or primarily by the violent conduct; the mere existence of stress alongside violence is insufficient without proven causation.

A finding of domestic violence can be made even where both parties engaged in forceful conduct during a conflict, provided the abusive conduct of one party in the form of harassment or intimidation is established.

Conduct that does not involve physical violence may still constitute domestic violence if it reasonably amounts to continued harassment or psychological intimidation from the perspective of the victim.

judges: Before: Deputy District Judge R So in Chambers (Not open to public)

principles.paraphrase_en: In matters concerning the custody, upbringing, or property of a minor, the court's paramount consideration is the welfare and best interests of the child.
The expressed views and wishes of children are a relevant factor but are not determinative; the court must independently assess what arrangements are in their best interests.
In considering a child's best interests, the court should recognize the importance of the child maintaining a relationship with both parents, implying an equality of opportunity where appropriate.
Parents should discuss and coordinate children's schedules and activities to allow for flexible arrangements that serve the child's best interests, particularly regarding access.

A standard direction for temporary removal of a child from the jurisdiction is intended to facilitate parental communication and coordination in making holiday plans that are in the child's best interests.

judges: Coram: Deputy District Judge S. Allison in Chambers (not open to public)

principles.principle_label: Parental consultation rights regardless of custody
principles.paraphrase_en: A parent retains the right to be informed and consulted about major decisions concerning their child's upbringing, such as education, irrespective of their custodial status. If disagreements arise with the custodial parent, the non-custodial parent may apply to the court for resolution.

A court must give real and meaningful consideration to the wishes of a child in custody or access proceedings, especially when the child is mature, thoughtful, and able to express their views rationally and with emotional understanding. Simply acknowledging these wishes and then disregarding them constitutes an error.

A court's decision to make 'no order' on an application is legally distinct from dismissing it. 'No order' means the court has declined to determine the application on its merits at that time, leaving it as an outstanding issue, whereas dismissal is an adjudication against the application.

When considering an order for therapeutic intervention (e.g., family therapy) against a child's objections, the court must conduct a balancing exercise. If the evidence suggests forcing such therapy may cause more harm than good to the child's welfare and existing familial relationships, the court may decline to order it.

principles.principle_label: Court's inherent jurisdiction to restrict applications
principles.paraphrase_en: The court possesses an inherent jurisdiction to impose restrictions on future applications, such as requiring a party to obtain the court's leave before filing a fresh application in family proceedings, in order to prevent abuse of process and protect the welfare of the children involved.

relationships.description: The present judgment cites this case as an example where the court changed a child's residence and suspended access to protect the child from unfounded allegations.

judges: Coram: Deputy District Judge Eugene Yim in Chambers (not open to public)

principles.principle_label: Best interests of the child as paramount consideration
principles.paraphrase_en: The court's primary and guiding principle in matters concerning the custody, upbringing, or welfare of a minor is to determine what is in the child's best interests.

principles.principle_label: No necessity for factual findings on parental allegations where shared care accepted
principles.paraphrase_en: Where both parties have accepted in their final proposals that the child will spend significant time with each parent, the court may find it unnecessary to make specific factual findings on mutual allegations of parental unsuitability.

principles.principle_label: Equal sharing of long school holidays as a guiding principle
principles.paraphrase_en: In structuring access arrangements, long school holidays should, in principle, be shared equally between the parents, with the division implemented in blocks corresponding to specific holiday periods.

principles.principle_label: Alternative sharing of singular public/school holidays
principles.paraphrase_en: Single-day public holidays and school holidays should be shared equally between parents on an alternating basis throughout the academic year.

principles.principle_label: Court's reliance on expert welfare reports
principles.paraphrase_en: The court may accept and rely on the recommendations and reasoned adjustments made by a responsible and conscientious social welfare officer in expert reports concerning child welfare and access arrangements.

judges: Coram: Her Honour Judge Sharon D. Melloy in Chambers (Not open to public)

principles.paraphrase_en: Evidence showing exposure to a drug can reasonably support an inference that the person consumed it, but such evidence alone cannot prove habitual use or addiction.
The court has authority to determine matters concerning the custody, upbringing, and property administration of a minor.

principles.principle_label: Use of non-statutory guidance in children's cases
principles.paraphrase_en: In children's proceedings, the court may consider checklists or factors from proposed legislation as a useful, non-exhaustive guide, even if they are not yet enacted, when assessing the child's best interests.
Joint custody orders are not exceptional in current practice, as they are generally accepted to be in the interests of children, even absent statutory reform seen in other jurisdictions.

judges: Coram : District Judge A. Tse in Chambers (Not Open to Public)

principles.principle_label: Assessment of parental conduct in custody disputes
principles.paraphrase_en: In assessing allegations of inappropriate parental conduct, the court must consider whether the behavior, such as the use of 'toilet humour' or physical contact like scratching, indicates a harmful or sexually deviant intention, rather than merely judging the social acceptability of the conduct.

A party dissatisfied with a single joint expert's report may be permitted to adduce further expert evidence if their reasons are not fanciful and it would be unjust, having regard to the overriding objective, to refuse such permission, particularly where substantial issues are at stake.

principles.principle_label: Primacy of the joint expert's report
principles.paraphrase_en: A report prepared by a single joint expert should generally constitute the evidence on the issues it covers, and a stiffer test requiring 'good reasons' applies for amplifying or challenging that report through cross-examination or additional evidence, beyond merely having non-fanciful reasons.
principles.paragraph_span: [Paragraph discussing Peet v Mid-Kent Healthcare Trust]

principles.paraphrase_en: When court-ordered supervision of child access is implemented, the supervisors' schedule and conduct should be governed by established guidelines, and the parties should not interfere with the supervisors' operational decisions.

principles.principle_label: Court's liberty to vary expert instructions
principles.paraphrase_en: The court may grant a party liberty to instruct a different expert to observe proceedings, such as child access sessions, replacing a previously appointed expert, with such change taking immediate effect.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from Daniels v Walker that permission for further expert evidence may be granted where a party's reasons are not fanciful and refusal would be unjust.
The judgment applies the principle from Peet that a joint expert's report is primary evidence and a stiffer test of 'good reasons' is needed to adduce additional evidence.

judges: Before: Hon Cheung and G Lam JJA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A custody decision is not discriminatory on the basis of gender if it is based on practical experience and the realities of a child's life, rather than on prejudice or stereotypes.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principle from R v Y (Custody) that the importance of a mother to a young child is a matter of practical reality, not discrimination.

judges: Coram : His Honour Judge S. Lo in Chambers (Not Open to Public)

principles.paraphrase_en: In matters concerning a minor's custody, upbringing, or property, the sole governing principle is that the child's welfare is the paramount consideration, and no presumptions apply.

principles.principle_label: No presumption in favour of mother
principles.paraphrase_en: There is no legal presumption favouring the mother in custody or upbringing matters, and such gender-based views are obsolete and impermissible.

When applying the welfare principle, the court must place competing parents on an equal footing, prohibiting any role or gender discrimination, reflecting modern social values on shared parental responsibility.
The paramount consideration is the child's best interests, not parental authority, which is a universally recognized modern principle.
principles.paragraph_hrase_en: The paramount consideration is the child's best interests, not parental authority, which is a universally recognized modern principle.

principles.paraphrase_en: In cases of parental alienation, the court has an obligation to respond with exceptional diligence and take effective measures, avoiding inaction which would reinforce the stronger party's position.

relationships.description: The judgment cites Re F to support the principle that the child's welfare is paramount and no presumptions apply.
The judgment cites H v N for the proposition that no presumption exists in favour of the mother.
The judgment applies the reasoning from PD v KWW regarding the equality of parents and the shift away from gender-based presumptions.

relationships.target_case_name: Gillick v West Norfolk and Wisbech Area Health Authority
relationships.description: The judgment cites Gillick via PD v KWW for the principle that the child's best interests, not parental authority, are paramount.

judges: Coram : His Honour Judge S. Lo in Chambers (Not Open to Public)

principles.paraphrase_en: When determining matters concerning a minor's custody, upbringing, or property, the court must apply the welfare principle as the paramount consideration.

The Family Court consistently applies a welfare checklist, adapted from section 1(3) of the English Children Act 1989, to local circumstances when assessing a child's best interests under the relevant ordinance.

relationships.description: The present judgment adopts the correct approach to the 'best interests' principle as set out by Jeremy Poon J in H v N.

judges: Coram: His Honour Judge S Lo in Chambers (Not Open to Public)

principles.principle_label: Joint custody orders in child's interests
principles.paraphrase_en: Orders for joint custody are not exceptional and are generally made when they are in the best interests of the child.
Educated and capable parents should communicate directly about their child's welfare using simple means like email, rather than through lawyers, to avoid unnecessary expense and delay.

principles.principle_label: Presumption of parental competence and cooperation
principles.paraphrase_en: Competent and loving parents are presumed to be able to make rational decisions in their child's interests and to cooperate on important upbringing matters.

principles.principle_label: Equality principle not applicable to access
principles.paraphrase_en: The 'Equality Principle' does not apply to determining child access arrangements, which is a distinct issue from custody.

principles.principle_label: Status quo as important consideration for access
principles.paraphrase_en: Maintaining the child's current living and access arrangements is a very important factor for the court when determining access.

judges: Coram: Her Honour Judge Thelma Kwan in Chambers (not open to public)

principles.paraphrase_en: Joint custody is the general and encouraged approach in child custody matters, as it serves the child's best interests by involving both parents in major decisions.

principles.principle_label: Insufficiency of poor communication to deny joint custody
principles.paraphrase_en: A lack of communication between parents is not, by itself, a sufficient reason to refuse an order for joint custody. One parent cannot obstruct the other's fundamental parental rights merely by asserting an inability or unwillingness to communicate.

A parent with joint custody has a positive duty to proactively and fully inform the other parent about the child's schooling (diaries, notices, events) and health matters, and cannot expect the other parent to discover this information independently after the fact.

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof for allegations affecting custody
principles.paraphrase_en: A party making serious allegations (such as abuse or stress caused by a custody order) that could affect custody arrangements must provide proof to support those contentions; unsupported allegations will be found not credible.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principle from H v A that lack of communication is insufficient to deny joint custody.

judges: Coram : Deputy District Judge Robin Egerton in Chambers (Not Open to Public)

principles.paraphrase_en: The child's best interests are the overriding and paramount consideration in decisions concerning custody, care and control, and relocation.
A parent's application to relocate a child must be genuine, realistic, and not motivated by an intention to exclude the other parent from the child's life.

principles.principle_label: No order as to costs in children matters
principles.paraphrase_en: In applications concerning children, the general practice and usual order is that there be no order as to costs, to encourage parents to focus on arrangements in the child's best interests.

relationships.description: The judgment cites this Court of Appeal case for the principle that the usual order in children matters should be no order as to costs.

judges: Coram: Deputy District Judge Peter Barnes in Chambers (Not open to public)

principles.paraphrase_en: When making orders concerning the custody or education of a child in matrimonial proceedings, the child's best interests are the paramount consideration that the court must apply.

principles.principle_label: Test for authorization of arrest in injunction
principles.paraphrase_en: An authorization for arrest can be attached to a court order only if the court is satisfied that the respondent has already caused actual bodily harm to the protected person, or the court reasonably believes the respondent is likely to cause such harm.
The same legal test used for initially authorizing an arrest under a court order applies when the court considers extending that authorization.

candidates.case_name: WKI also known as IWKV v. CKW also known as WCKW
judges: Coram: Deputy District Judge Theresa Chow in Chambers (not open to public)

principles.paraphrase_en: In matters concerning the custody or upbringing of a minor, the child's welfare must be the court's paramount consideration.

principles.principle_label: Use of welfare checklist as guideline
principles.paraphrase_en: The court may consider the welfare checklist from the English Children Act 1989 as a useful guideline when applying the welfare principle, even though similar local legislation is pending.

principles.principle_label: Admissibility of covert surveillance evidence in children proceedings
principles.paraphrase_en: Covert surveillance evidence is permissible in children proceedings in appropriate cases, with the decision depending on the special circumstances of the individual case and the best interests of the child as the guiding principle.

principles.principle_label: Striking out scandalous or irrelevant material from affidavits
principles.paraphrase_en: The court has the power to strike out material from an affidavit that is scandalous, irrelevant, or otherwise oppressive.

The degree of relevance required for admissibility is not fixed and may involve balancing the evidence's relevance against considerations such as inconvenience, expense, delay, or oppression, which is akin to exercising discretion.

relationships.target_case_name: Mohammad Amjad v. John M Pickavant & CO
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case as discussing the principles for striking out scandalous or irrelevant material from affidavits.
The judgment applies the presumption from this case that competent, loving parents with sufficient objectivity can cooperate on important child upbringing matters.

judges: Before: Deputy District Judge R So in Chambers (Not open to public)

principles.paraphrase_en: In matters concerning the custody, upbringing, or access to a child, the court's first and paramount consideration is the best interests of the child.

principles.principle_label: Consideration of child's wishes and feelings
principles.paraphrase_en: When determining the child's best interests, the court must give due consideration to the child's wishes and feelings, having regard to the child's age, understanding, and the circumstances of the case.

principles.principle_label: Nature of care and control and access
principles.paraphrase_en: The labels 'care and control' and 'access' are functional. During access periods, the parent with access assumes care and control of the child. The parent with substantial access must be consulted on arrangements affecting their access time.

Pre-determined, fixed dates for long school holiday access may not serve the child's best interests, as children's schedules change with age and activities. Parents should communicate and agree on dates within a reasonable timeframe before the holiday.
Parents are expected to use their best endeavours to communicate and reach agreements regarding child arrangements, shifting focus from mutual blame to the child's growth and development.

candidates.case_name: BKGGB also known as NGGB v. BKND
judges: Coram: Deputy District Judge Thelma Kwan in Chambers (not open to public)

principles.principle_label: Paramountcy of child's welfare in relocation
principles.paraphrase_en: In cases concerning the relocation of a child, whether external or internal, the only legal test is the paramount principle of the child's welfare. This involves a holistic, non-linear balancing exercise of all relevant factors, assisted by analogy with the welfare checklist.

principles.principle_label: Status of Payne v Payne guidance
principles.paraphrase_en: The guidance and factors set out in Payne v Payne are not prescriptive rules of law. The only principle of law from that case is the paramountcy of the child's best interests; the rest is guidance to be applied flexibly and with caution, particularly to ensure a gender-neutral approach.
Orders for joint custody are not exceptional and are, in principle, considered to be in the interests of children.

In cases where the care of a child is shared between parents, the court should avoid becoming bogged down in arguments over labels attached to the care arrangement and should focus on the practical realities.

relationships.target_case_name: ZJ v XWN (Leave to Appeal: Child Relocation)
relationships.description: The present judgment relies on this Court of Appeal case to confirm that the only legal principle from Payne v Payne is the paramountcy of the child's welfare.
The judgment discusses the status of the guidance from this case, noting its factors may have limited utility and should not be applied prescriptively.

relationships.target_case_name: K v K (Children: Permanent Removal from Jurisdiction)
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case, specifically the view of Black LJ, regarding the approach to labeling shared care arrangements.
This authority was reviewed by the Court of Appeal in ZJ v XWN, which the present judgment follows.

candidates.case_name: C, RYL (formerly known as S, R) v. C, S
judges: Coram: Her Honour Judge Sharon D. Melloy in Chambers (Not open to public)

principles.paraphrase_en: A court has the authority to vary an existing custody order, including altering which parent is granted care and control of a child and determining the child's primary residence.
The court may order joint custody of a child, separating the legal concept of custody from the practical arrangements of care and control and access.
A court order varying care and control can mandate the child's move to a new residence to take effect immediately upon the order being made.

judges: Coram : Her Honour Judge Grace Chan in Chambers (paper disposal)

principles.principle_label: Welfare of the child as paramount consideration
principles.paraphrase_en: In proceedings concerning the variation of custody or access orders, the court's primary and overriding consideration is the welfare of the child.

The court is generally reluctant to vary existing custody and access arrangements, especially where they were established by a full trial or subsequent consent order, unless there is a material change in circumstances affecting the child's welfare.

principles.principle_label: Child's views as a relevant factor
principles.paraphrase_en: The expressed wishes, feelings, and reactions of the children, particularly when documented in professional reports, are important factors for the court to consider when assessing their welfare in custody and access disputes.

principles.principle_label: Use of expert and welfare reports
principles.paraphrase_en: In complex family disputes involving children, the court may direct the preparation of social investigation and clinical psychological reports to obtain independent, expert assessments of the children's circumstances and needs to inform its decision.

candidates.case_name: LUI MING LOK v. NG IM FONG LORETTA
judges: Before: Hon Cheung, Yuen and Chow JJA in Court

principles.principle_label: Void vs voidable marriage for mental incapacity
principles.paraphrase_en: Under modern matrimonial legislation (specifically by the time of the Matrimonial Causes Ordinance 1973), lack of consent due to unsoundness of mind or mental disorder can only render a marriage voidable, not void.

A marriage involving an insane person may not be declared null and void if the marriage is beneficial to that person, such as where the other spouse provides care, and third parties should not be permitted to interfere with it.

principles.principle_label: Party's right to assert own incapacity
principles.paraphrase_en: A party may bring proceedings to annul a marriage on the ground of their own mental incapacity at the time of the marriage, as such incapacity invalidates the marriage contract due to want of consent.

In statutory interpretation, where a general enactment covers a situation for which specific provision is made elsewhere in the legislation, it is presumed the situation is intended to be dealt with by the specific provision.

relationships.description: The judgment cites Browning v. Reane for the principle that a beneficial marriage to an insane person may not be declared void and to illustrate the historical common law rule.
The judgment cites Turner v Meyers for the principle that a party can assert their own past mental incapacity to annul a marriage for want of consent.
